subject: Physics
q: In materials like aluminium and copper,  the correct order of magnitude of various elastic modulii is :
question: In materials like aluminium and copper,  the correct order of magnitude of various elastic modulii is :
options: Young's modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> shear modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> bulk modulii
Bulk modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> shear modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> Young's modulii
Shear modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> Young's modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> bulk modulii
Bulk modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> Young's modulii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"> <mo><</mo> </math> shear modulii
solution: For aluminium and copper magnitude of Shear modulii is less than Young's modulii and bulk modulii is maximum.

subject: Physics
q: Wires W1 and W2 are made of same material having the breaking stress of 1.25×109 N m-2. W1 and W2 have cross-sectional area of 8×10-7 m2 and 4×10-7 m2, respectively. Masses of 20 kg and 10 kg hang from them as shown in the figure. The maximum mass that can be placed in the pan without breaking the wires is _____kg (Use g=10 m s-2
question: Wires W1 and W2 are made of same material having the breaking stress of 1.25×109 N m-2. W1 and W2 have cross-sectional area of 8×10-7 m2 and 4×10-7 m2, respectively. Masses of 20 kg and 10 kg hang from them as shown in the figure. The maximum mass that can be placed in the pan without breaking the wires is _____kg (Use g=10 m s-2
solution:
F1max= stress × area

=1.25×8×102=1000 N

F2max=500 N

F2max=(10+M)g=500 N

M=40 kg

subject: Physics
q: A force is applied to a steel wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, rigidly clamped at one end. As a result elongation in the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. If same force is applied to another steel wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of double the length and a diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math> times that of the wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the elongation in the wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be (wires having uniform circular cross sections)
question: A force is applied to a steel wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, rigidly clamped at one end. As a result elongation in the wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. If same force is applied to another steel wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of double the length and a diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math> times that of the wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the elongation in the wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be (wires having uniform circular cross sections)
solution:
As we know, Y=FA∆ll

⇒F=YAl∆l

For both cases force applied is the same, therefore

YA∆ll1=YA∆ll2

⇒∆l2∆l1=A1A2×l2l1

⇒∆l20.2=πR2π2.4R2×21

⇒∆l2=6.9×10-2 mm

subject: Physics
q: A square aluminium (shear modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>) slab of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is subjected to a shearing force (on its narrow face) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The lower edge is riveted to the floor. The displacement of the upper edge is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: A square aluminium (shear modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>) slab of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is subjected to a shearing force (on its narrow face) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The lower edge is riveted to the floor. The displacement of the upper edge is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Shearing stress is given by FA=ηxl

Thus, displacement is x=FlAη

⇒x=18×104×60×10-260×10-2×15×10-2×25×109

=48×10-6 m=48 μm

subject: Physics
q: Two slabs with square cross section of different materials $(1,2)$ with equal sides $(l)$ and thickness $\mathrm{d}_1$ and $\mathrm{d}_2$ such that $\mathrm{d}_2=2 \mathrm{~d}_1$ and $l \gt \mathrm{d}_2$. Considering lower edges of these slabs are fixed to the floor, we apply equal shearing force on the narrow faces. The angle of deformation is $\theta_2=2 \theta_1$. If the shear moduli of material 1 is $4 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, then shear moduli of material 2 is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, where value of $x$ is ________.
question: Two slabs with square cross section of different materials $(1,2)$ with equal sides $(l)$ and thickness $\mathrm{d}_1$ and $\mathrm{d}_2$ such that $\mathrm{d}_2=2 \mathrm{~d}_1$ and $l \gt \mathrm{d}_2$. Considering lower edges of these slabs are fixed to the floor, we apply equal shearing force on the narrow faces. The angle of deformation is $\theta_2=2 \theta_1$. If the shear moduli of material 1 is $4 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, then shear moduli of material 2 is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, where value of $x$ is ________.
solution:
Deformation angle
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 \theta_1=\theta_2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \frac{\sigma_1}{\eta_1}=\frac{\sigma_2}{\eta_2}
\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\ell \mathrm{~d}_1 \eta_1}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\ell \mathrm{d}_2 \eta_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \eta_2=\frac{\eta_1}{4}=1 \times 10^9 \Rightarrow x=1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The length of metallic wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> when tension in it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. It is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when the tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The original length of the wire will be :
question: The length of metallic wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> when tension in it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. It is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when the tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The original length of the wire will be :
solution:
Assuming Hooke's law to be valid

T∝Δℓ

T=kΔℓ

Let,ℓ0= natural length (original length) ⇒T=kℓ-ℓ0

so, T1=kℓ1-ℓ0 & T2=kℓ2-ℓ0

⇒T1T2=ℓ1-ℓ0ℓ2-ℓ0

⇒ℓ0=T2ℓ1-T1ℓ2 T2-T1

q: Young's moduli of two wires <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are in the ratio <math><mi> </mi><mn>7</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math> . Wire <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and has radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Wire <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and has radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the two wires stretch by the same length for a given load, the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is close to:
question: Young's moduli of two wires <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are in the ratio <math><mi> </mi><mn>7</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math> . Wire <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and has radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Wire <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and has radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the two wires stretch by the same length for a given load, the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is close to:
solution:
Strain = ∆l/l
Stress =F/A
Young’s modulus =StressStrain=F/A∆l/l=FAl∆l
YAYB=lAAAABlB=74
2πrA2πrB21.5=74
rA2=21.5×7×2 mm2
rA=1.7 mm

subject: Physics
q: The increase in pressure required to decrease the volume of a water sample by $0.2 \%$ is $\mathrm{P} \times 10^5 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. Bulk modulus of water is $2.15 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. The value of P is $\ldots\ldots$
question: The increase in pressure required to decrease the volume of a water sample by $0.2 \%$ is $\mathrm{P} \times 10^5 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. Bulk modulus of water is $2.15 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. The value of P is $\ldots\ldots$
solution:
Since bulk modulus is given as
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{B}=\frac{-\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{~V}}\right)} \\
& 2.15 \times 10^9=\frac{-\Delta \mathrm{P}}{-\left(\frac{0.2}{100}\right)} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{P}=2.15 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \\
& =4.3 \times 10^6=43 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
As shown in the figure, in an experiment to determine Young's modulus of a wire, the extension-load curve is plotted. The curve is a straight line passing through the origin and makes an angle of 45° with the load axis. The length of wire is 62.8 cm and its diameter is 4 mm. The Young's modulus is found to be  x×104 N m-2

The value of x is _______.
question:
As shown in the figure, in an experiment to determine Young's modulus of a wire, the extension-load curve is plotted. The curve is a straight line passing through the origin and makes an angle of 45° with the load axis. The length of wire is 62.8 cm and its diameter is 4 mm. The Young's modulus is found to be  x×104 N m-2

The value of x is _______.
solution:
Given here, L=62.8 cm and d=4 mm.

From the given graph, we can see that extension in wire is linearly dependent on force applied. Slope of the curve is tan45°=1.

So, F=ΔL

Young's modulus is given by Y=FLAΔL, where, A is area of wire.

⇒Y=LA

⇒Y=62.8×10-2π2×10-32

⇒Y=5×104 N m-2

subject: Physics
q:
The volume contraction of a solid copper cube of edge length 10 cm , when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of $7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Pa}$, would be ____ $\mathrm{mm}^3$.
(Given bulk modulus of copper $=1.4 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
question:
The volume contraction of a solid copper cube of edge length 10 cm , when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of $7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Pa}$, would be ____ $\mathrm{mm}^3$.
(Given bulk modulus of copper $=1.4 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{V}=\frac{7 \times 10^6}{1.4 \times 10^{11}} \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^3 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{~V}=50 \mathrm{~mm}^3\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A cube has side length $5$ cm and modulus of rigidity $10^5$ N/m$^2$. The displacement produced by a force of $10$ N in the upper face of cube is _____ mm.
question: A cube has side length $5$ cm and modulus of rigidity $10^5$ N/m$^2$. The displacement produced by a force of $10$ N in the upper face of cube is _____ mm.
solution:
Given:
Side length of the cube, $L = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
Modulus of rigidity, $\eta = 10^5$ N/m$^2$
Force applied on the upper face, $F = 10$ N

Area of the upper face, $A = L^2 = (0.05)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m$^2$

Shear stress is given by:
$\text{Stress} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{10}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4000$ N/m$^2$

Shear strain is given by:
$\text{Strain} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L}$

Modulus of rigidity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain:
$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{F/A}{\Delta x/L}$

Rearranging for displacement $\Delta x$:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot \eta}$

Substituting the values:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{10 \times 0.05}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^5}$

$\Delta x = \dfrac{0.5}{250} = 0.002$ m

Converting to millimeters:
$\Delta x = 0.002 \times 1000 = 2$ mm

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: A cube of metal is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>G</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>. The percentage change in the length of the side of the cube is close to : (Given bulk modulus of metal, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>)
question: A cube of metal is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>G</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>. The percentage change in the length of the side of the cube is close to : (Given bulk modulus of metal, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>)
solution:
B=-∆P∆VV
∆VV=-∆PB
Magnitude is given by,
∆VV=∆PB

Volume in terms of length can be represented as,

3×∆LL=∆PB

Therefore,

∆LL=13×∆PB

Substituting the values we get,
∆LL=13×4×1098×1010=160

Percantage change is,
160×100=53=1.67

subject: Physics
q: The two wires $A$ and $B$ of equal cross-section but of different materials are joined together. The ratio of Young's modulus of wire $A$ and wire $B$ is $20/11$. When the joined wire is kept under certain tension the elongations in the wires $A$ and $B$ are equal. If the length of wire $A$ is $2.2$ m, then the length of wire $B$ is _______ m.
question: The two wires $A$ and $B$ of equal cross-section but of different materials are joined together. The ratio of Young's modulus of wire $A$ and wire $B$ is $20/11$. When the joined wire is kept under certain tension the elongations in the wires $A$ and $B$ are equal. If the length of wire $A$ is $2.2$ m, then the length of wire $B$ is _______ m.
solution:
Given that the wires have equal cross-sectional area $A$ and are under the same tension $F$, the elongation $\Delta L$ is given by:

$\Delta L = \dfrac{F L}{A Y}$

Since the elongations in both wires are equal ($\Delta L_A = \Delta L_B$):

$\dfrac{F L_A}{A Y_A} = \dfrac{F L_B}{A Y_B}$

$\dfrac{L_A}{Y_A} = \dfrac{L_B}{Y_B}$

$L_B = L_A \times \dfrac{Y_B}{Y_A}$

Substituting the given values $L_A = 2.2$ m and $\dfrac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \dfrac{20}{11}$:

$L_B = 2.2 \times \dfrac{11}{20}$

$L_B = \dfrac{24.2}{20} = 1.21$ m

Answer: $1.21$

q: A uniformly tapering conical wire is made from a material of Young's modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> and has a normal, unextended length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. The radii, at the upper and lower ends of this conical wire, have values <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math>, respectively. The equilibrium extended length, of this wire, would equal:
question: A uniformly tapering conical wire is made from a material of Young's modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> and has a normal, unextended length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. The radii, at the upper and lower ends of this conical wire, have values <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math>, respectively. The upper end of the wire is fixed to a rigid support and a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is suspended from its lower end. The equilibrium extended length, of this wire, would equal:
solution:

For a small section

FA=Ydldx

⟹∫0LdL=∫0LMgdxπ2RLx×R2Y



∆L=MgπY-12RxL+R0L×L2R

=MgL3πR2Y

L f =L+ΔL=L[ 1+ mg 3π R 2 Y ]

q:
The depth below the surface of sea to which a rubber ball be taken so as to decrease its volume by 0.02% is _____ m.

(Take density of sea water =103 kg m-3, Bulk modulus of rubber =9×108 N m-2, and g=10 m s-2)
question:

(Take density of sea water =103 kg m-3, Bulk modulus of rubber =9×108 N m-2, and g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Bulk modulus is given by, B=-∆P∆VV

⇒∆P=-B∆VV

⇒ρgh=-B∆VV

⇒103×10×h=-9×108×-0.02100

⇒h=18 m

subject: Physics
q: The Young's modulus of a steel wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>11</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . The wire is suspended from its support on a given planet. A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to the free end of the wire. The acceleration due to gravity on the planet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> of its value on the earth. The elongation of wire is (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> on the earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> ):
question: The Young's modulus of a steel wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>11</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . The wire is suspended from its support on a given planet. A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to the free end of the wire. The acceleration due to gravity on the planet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> of its value on the earth. The elongation of wire is (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> on the earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> ):
solution:
The external force or tension F acting on the wire is its own weight. It is given by

 F= mg=4 kg×104 m s-2=10 N

The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire is given by

Y= FA∆LL= FL∆LA..........................(1)

where, A is the cross-sectional area, L is the original length and ∆L is the elongation of the wire.

Substitute the given values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required elongation of the wire.

2×1011 N/m2= 10 N×6 m∆L×3 mm2×1 m2106 mm2∆L= 10 N×6 m2×1011 N/m2×3 mm2×1 m2106 mm2= 0.0001 m×103 mm1 m= 0.1 mm

subject: Physics
q:
The force required to stretch a wire of cross-section 1 cm2 to double its length will be :

(Given Yong's modulus of the wire =2×1011 N m-2)
question:
The force required to stretch a wire of cross-section 1 cm2 to double its length will be :

(Given Yong's modulus of the wire =2×1011 N m-2)
solution:
The wire is stretched to double its length. Therefore, if the length of the wore was l, change in the length ∆l=l.

Applying Hooke's law,

Y=stressstrain=FlA∆l⇒F=YA∆ll=YA⇒F=2×1011×1×10-4=2×107 N

=2×1011×10-42l-ll

=2×107 N

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct relationship between Poisson ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>σ</mi></mfenced></math>, bulk modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and modulus of rigidity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>η</mi></mfenced></math> of a given solid object:
question: Choose the correct relationship between Poisson ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>σ</mi></mfenced></math>, bulk modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and modulus of rigidity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>η</mi></mfenced></math> of a given solid object:
solution:
The relations of Young's modulus, Y, bulk modulus, K, the modulus of rigidity, η and Poisson's ratio, σ are given as

Y=3K(1-2σ) …1 and 

Y=2η1+σ …2

From equation 1 and 2, we have

⇒2η1+σ=3K1-2σ

⇒σ=3K-2η6K+2η

subject: Physics
q: The area of cross-section of a railway track is 0.01 m2. The temperature variation is 10 °C. Coefficient of linear expansion of material of track is 10-5 C-1∘. The energy stored per meter in the track is J m-1. (Young's modulus of material of track is 1011 N m-2)
question: The area of cross-section of a railway track is 0.01 m2. The temperature variation is 10 °C. Coefficient of linear expansion of material of track is 10-5 C-1∘. The energy stored per meter in the track is J m-1. (Young's modulus of material of track is 1011 N m-2)
solution:
Elastic energy =Y2(strain)2× Area × length

⇒ Elastic energy per unit length =Y2(strain)2× Area

(strain =Δℓℓ=αΔT=10-5×10=10-4)
=10112×10-42×10-2=5 J m-1

subject: Physics
q: A lift of mass $1600$ kg is supported by thick iron wire. If the maximum stress which the wire can withstand is $4 \times 10^8$ N/m$^2$ and its radius is $4$ mm, then maximum acceleration the lift can take is _______ m/s$^2$. (take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$ and $\pi = 3.14$)
question: A lift of mass $1600$ kg is supported by thick iron wire. If the maximum stress which the wire can withstand is $4 \times 10^8$ N/m$^2$ and its radius is $4$ mm, then maximum acceleration the lift can take is _______ m/s$^2$. (take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$ and $\pi = 3.14$)
solution:
Maximum stress $\sigma_{max} = 4 \times 10^8$ N/m$^2$

Radius of wire $r = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m

Mass of lift $m = 1600$ kg

Maximum tension the wire can withstand is given by:

$T_{max} = \sigma_{max} \times \pi r^2$

$T_{max} = 4 \times 10^8 \times 3.14 \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^2$

$T_{max} = 4 \times 10^8 \times 3.14 \times 16 \times 10^{-6} = 20096$ N

When the lift accelerates upwards with maximum acceleration $a$, the tension in the wire is:

$T_{max} = m(g + a)$

$20096 = 1600(10 + a)$

$10 + a = \dfrac{20096}{1600} = 12.56$

$a = 12.56 - 10 = 2.56$ m/s$^2$

Answer: $2.56$

subject: Physics
q: The length of a wire becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> tension are applied respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>11</mn><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, then the natural length of wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Here the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: The length of a wire becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> tension are applied respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>11</mn><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, then the natural length of wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Here the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
Young's modulus is given by Y=FA∆ll

⇒FA=YΔll⇒Δl=FlAY

⇒l+Δl=l1⇒F1LAY+L=LF1AY+1=l1   ...(i)

Similarly,

l2=F2AY+1L

⇒10l2=11l1

It is given that 10l1=11l2

⇒10×LF2AY+1=11LF1AY+1

⇒10F2AY+10=11F1AY+11

Substituting F1=100 N, F2=120 N

⇒1200AY−1100AY=1⇒100AY=1

⇒1AY=1100

Using equation (i),

 L=l1F1AY+1=l1100100+1=l12

subject: Physics
q: Steel ruptures when a shear of $3.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ is applied. The force needed to punch a $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ diameter hole in a steel sheet $0.3 \mathrm{~cm}$ thick is nearly:
question: Steel ruptures when a shear of $3.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ is applied. The force needed to punch a $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ diameter hole in a steel sheet $0.3 \mathrm{~cm}$ thick is nearly:
solution: Shearing strain is created along the side surface of the punched disk. Note that the forces exerted on the disk are exerted along the circumference of the disk, and the total force exerted on its center only. Let us assume that the shearing stress along the side surface of the disk is uniform, then $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{F}>& \int_{\text {surface }} \mathrm{dF}_{\max }=\int_{\text {surface }} \sigma_{\max } \mathrm{dA}=\sigma_{\max } \int_{\text {surface }} \mathrm{dA} \\ &=\int \sigma_{\max } \cdot \mathrm{A}=\sigma_{\max } \cdot 2 \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}}{2}\right) \mathrm{h} \\ &=3.5 \times 10^8 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-2}\right) \times 0.3 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \pi \\ &=3.297 \times 10^4 \simeq 3.3 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: The elastic behaviour of material for linear stress and linear strain, is shown in the figure. The energy density for a linear strain of 5×10-4 is _____  kJ m-3. Assume that material is elastic upto the linear strain of 5×10-4,
question: The elastic behaviour of material for linear stress and linear strain, is shown in the figure. The energy density for a linear strain of 5×10-4 is _____  kJ m-3. Assume that material is elastic upto the linear strain of 5×10-4,
solution:
From the above graph, we can find that when the strain is 5×10-4 the stress is 100 MPa. We know that energy density is

U=12×stress×strain⇒U=12×100×106×5×10-4

⇒U=2.5×104 J m-3=25 kJ m-3

subject: Physics
q: Two wires of same length and radius are joined end to end and loaded. The Young's moduli of the materials of the two wires are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The combination behaves as a single wire then its Young's modulus is:
question: Two wires of same length and radius are joined end to end and loaded. The Young's moduli of the materials of the two wires are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The combination behaves as a single wire then its Young's modulus is:
solution:
In series combination, Δl=ℓ1+ℓ2

Y=F/AΔℓ/ℓ⇒Δℓ=FℓAY

⇒Δℓ∝ℓY

Equivalent length of rod after joining is =2ℓ

As, lengths are same and force is also same in series

Δℓ=Δℓ1+Δℓ2

ℓeqYeq=ℓY1+ℓY2⇒2ℓY=ℓY1+ℓY2

∴ Y=2Y1Y2Y1+Y2

subject: Physics
q: A sample of a liquid is kept at 1 atm. It is compressed to 5 atm which leads to change of volume of $0.8 \mathrm{~cm}^3$. If the bulk modulus of the liquid is 2 GPa , the initial volume of the liquid was _______ litre. $\left(\right.$ Take $\left.1 \mathrm{~atm}=10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}\right)$
question: A sample of a liquid is kept at 1 atm. It is compressed to 5 atm which leads to change of volume of $0.8 \mathrm{~cm}^3$. If the bulk modulus of the liquid is 2 GPa , the initial volume of the liquid was _______ litre. $\left(\right.$ Take $\left.1 \mathrm{~atm}=10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}\right)$
solution:
Given, Initial pressure of liquid $\left(P_i\right)=1 \mathrm{~atm}$
Final pressure of liquid $\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)=5 \mathrm{~atm}$
Change in pressure $(\mathrm{dP})=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}=4 \mathrm{~atm}$
$=4 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}$
Change in volume $(\mathrm{dV})=-0.8 \mathrm{~cm}^3$
Bulk modulus $(B)=2 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~Pa}$
Now, $B=\frac{-d P}{(d V / V)} \Rightarrow V=-B\left(\frac{d V}{d P}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow & V=-2 \times 10^9 \times \frac{\left(-0.8 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{4 \times 10^5} \\ & =4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3=4 \text { litre }
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If the length of a wire is made double and radius is halved of its respective values. Then, the Young's modulus of the material of the wire will :
question: If the length of a wire is made double and radius is halved of its respective values. Then, the Young's modulus of the material of the wire will :
solution:
Young's modulus is the ratio of stress to strain.

Young's modulus is a property of a material, and it does not depend on the geometry i.e. length and the radius of the wire. Therefore, the Young's modulus of the wire will remain same.

subject: Physics
q: The value of tension in a long thin metal wire has been changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The lengths of the metal wire at two different values of tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ℓ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ℓ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively. The actual length of the metal wire is:
question: The value of tension in a long thin metal wire has been changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The lengths of the metal wire at two different values of tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ℓ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ℓ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively. The actual length of the metal wire is:
solution:
Y=FLAΔL
⇒Y=T1ℓ0 Aℓ1-ℓ0=T2ℓ0 Aℓ2-ℓ0
1=T1ℓ2-ℓ0T2ℓ1-ℓ0
T2ℓ1-T2ℓ0=T1ℓ2-T1ℓ0
T1-T2ℓ0=T1ℓ2-T2ℓ1
ℓ0=T1ℓ2-T2ℓ1 T1-T2

subject: Physics
q: A uniform wire (Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ ) is subjected to longitudinal tensile stress of $5 \times 10^7 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. If the overall volume change in the wire is $0.02 \%$, the fractional decrease in the radius of the wire is close to:
question: A uniform wire (Young's modulus $2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ ) is subjected to longitudinal tensile stress of $5 \times 10^7 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. If the overall volume change in the wire is $0.02 \%$, the fractional decrease in the radius of the wire is close to:
solution:
Given, $y=2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Stress }\left(\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{A}}\right)=5 \times 10^7 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{V}=0.02 \%=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^3 \\
& \frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}=?
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \gamma=\frac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }} \Rightarrow \operatorname{strain}\left(\frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell_0}\right)=\frac{\gamma}{\text { stress }} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{V}=2 \pi \mathrm{r} \ell_0 \Delta \mathrm{r}-\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \Delta \ell
\end{aligned}
$$
From eqns (i) and (ii) putting the value of $\Delta \ell, \ell_0$ and $\Delta \mathrm{V}$ and solving we get
$$
\frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}=0.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is hanging from a fixed support. The length changes to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are suspended respectively from its free end. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is equal to
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is hanging from a fixed support. The length changes to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are suspended respectively from its free end. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
From Hooke's law, ∆L=FLAY⇒∆L∝F. Therefore,

L2-LL1-L=m2gm1g=21  ⇒L=2L1-L2

subject: Physics
q: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. However wire 1 has crosssectional area $A$ and wire-2 has cross-sectional area $3 A$. If the length of wire 1 increases by $\Delta x$ on applying force $F$, how much force is needed to stretch wire 2 by the same amount?
question: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. However wire 1 has crosssectional area $A$ and wire-2 has cross-sectional area $3 A$. If the length of wire 1 increases by $\Delta x$ on applying force $F$, how much force is needed to stretch wire 2 by the same amount?
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}_1 \ell_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \ell_2 \Rightarrow \ell_2=\frac{\mathrm{A}_1 \ell_1}{\mathrm{~A}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{A} \times \ell_1}{3 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\ell_1}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=3 \\
& \Delta \mathrm{x}_1=\frac{\mathrm{F}_1}{\mathrm{~A} \gamma} \times \ell_1 \\
& \Delta \mathrm{x}_2=\frac{\mathrm{F}_2}{3 \mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_2 \\
& \text { Here } \Delta \mathrm{x}_1=\Delta \mathrm{x}_2 \\
& \frac{\mathrm{F}_2}{3 \mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_2=\frac{\mathrm{F}_1}{\mathrm{~A} \gamma} \ell_1 \\
& \left.\mathrm{~F}_2=3 \mathrm{~F}_1 \times \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=3 \mathrm{~F}_1 \times 3=\mathrm{i}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The density and breaking stress of a wire are $6 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $1.2 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ respectively. The wire is suspended from a rigid support on a planet where acceleration due to gravity is $\frac{1}{3}^{\text {rd }}$ of the value on the surface of earth. The maximum length of the wire with breaking is _____ m (take, $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ).
question: The density and breaking stress of a wire are $6 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $1.2 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ respectively. The wire is suspended from a rigid support on a planet where acceleration due to gravity is $\frac{1}{3}^{\text {rd }}$ of the value on the surface of earth. The maximum length of the wire with breaking is _____ m (take, $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ).
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{mg} \\ & \sigma=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{A}} \\ & \frac{(\sigma \mathrm{A} \ell) \mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{A}} \\ & \Rightarrow \ell=\frac{\sigma}{\rho \mathrm{g}}=\frac{1.2 \times 10^8 \times 3}{6 \times 10^4 \times 10}=600\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two wires A and B are made of same material having ratio of lengths $\frac{L_A}{L_B}=\frac{1}{3}$ and their diameters ratio $\frac{d_A}{d_B}=2$. If both the wires are stretched using same force, what would be the ratio of their respective elongations?
question: Two wires A and B are made of same material having ratio of lengths $\frac{L_A}{L_B}=\frac{1}{3}$ and their diameters ratio $\frac{d_A}{d_B}=2$. If both the wires are stretched using same force, what would be the ratio of their respective elongations?
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}}=2$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}$
Given, $F_A=F_B$ and $Y_A=Y_B$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}}{\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\frac{\pi}{4} \mathrm{~d}_{\mathrm{B}}^2}{\frac{\pi}{4} \mathrm{~d}_{\mathrm{A}}^2}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)^2$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{12}$

subject: Physics
q: A 3 m long wire of radius 3 mm shows an extension of 0.1 mm when loaded vertically by a mass of 50 kg in an experiment to determine Young's modulus. The value of Young's modulus of the wire as per this experiment is $\mathrm{P} \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, where the value of P is: (Take $\left.\mathrm{g}=3 \pi \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
question: A 3 m long wire of radius 3 mm shows an extension of 0.1 mm when loaded vertically by a mass of 50 kg in an experiment to determine Young's modulus. The value of Young's modulus of the wire as per this experiment is $\mathrm{P} \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$, where the value of P is: (Take $\left.\mathrm{g}=3 \pi \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{50 \mathrm{~g}}{\pi \mathrm{r}^2}=\mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell} \\ & \frac{50 \times 3 \pi}{\pi \times\left(3 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2}=\mathrm{P} \times 10^{11} \times \frac{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}=\frac{50 \times 3 \times 3}{3^2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{11} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & \mathrm{P}=5\end{aligned}$
